HOW TO SUBMIT PUBLIC COMMENTS:
In Person:
Anyone who desires to address the City Council during
Open Forum
, must complete and file a pink speaker’s card with the City Clerk
prior
to the City Council’s consideration of the item.
Online speakers:
must raise their hand when instructed by the clerk. Once the City Clerk has announced the item and given a count of the number of total speakers, no person shall be permitted to speak on the item other than those persons who have submitted their names to the City Clerk or those that have their hand raised.
The amount of time allotted to individual speakers shall be determined based on the total number of persons requesting to speak during this item. The time allocation for each speaker will be as follows: 15 or fewer speakers, a maximum of 2 minutes; 16 to 24 speakers, a maximum of 1 and one-half minutes; and 25 or more speakers, a maximum of 1 minute.
NON-PUBLIC HEARING ITEMS LISTED ON THE AGENDA: Each speaker will be allowed up to TWO minutes to address the Council.  PUBLIC HEARING ITEMS: Speakers are allowed up to THREE (3) minutes to address the Council.
Via Zoom
:
Anyone who desires to address the City Council on items appearing on the agenda,
including Open Forum
, must raise their hand once public comment is open.
The public speakers attending in person will be called first, followed by Zoom participants (when appropriate).  Only those public speakers that have their hand raised at the appropriate time will be recognized.

**
The mayor will announce the agenda item number and open public comment when appropriate.
Via mail:
received by 1:00 p.m. the day of the meeting, sent to 450 Civic Center Plaza, 3rd Floor, Office of the Clerk, Richmond, CA 94804.
Via eComments
: eComments are available once an agenda is published. Locate the meeting in "upcoming meetings" and click the comment bubble icon. Click on the item you wish to comment on. eComments can be submitted when the agenda is published and until the conclusion of public comments for the agenda item. Email your comment to
[email protected]
should you have difficulty submitting an eComment during a meeting.
Via email:
to
[email protected]
by 1:00 p.m
. the day of the meeting.
Emails
MUST
contain in the subject line 1) public comments – Open Session prior to Closed Session; 2) public comments – Open Forum; or 3) public comments agenda item #__ [include the agenda item number].
Properly labeled public comments
will be considered a public record, put into the official meeting record, available after the meeting as supplemental materials, and will be posted as an attachment to the meeting minutes when the minutes are posted:
http://www.ci.richmond.ca.us/Archive.aspx?AMID=31
The City cannot guarantee that its network and/or the site will be uninterrupted. To ensure that the City Council receives your comments, you are strongly encouraged to submit your comments in writing in advance of the meeting.
Procedures for Removing Items from the Consent Calendar
:
Members of the public who request to remove an item from the consent calendar must first discuss the item with a staff member by phone or a back-and-forth email discussion, and state the name of the staff member when requesting removal of the item from the consent calendar
(by 2 p.m. the day of the meeting)
.  The procedures for discussion do not apply to items sponsored by the mayor or councilmembers.
Any member of the City Council who would like to remove an item from the consent calendar must notify the appropriate staff person and the City Clerk’s Office prior to the meeting. Although members of the City Council are encouraged to ask questions and share concerns with staff ahead of the meeting, they are not required to do so.
The Clerk’s Office must be informed of any requests to remove items from the Consent Calendar. Items removed from the Consent Calendar shall be placed at the end of the agenda for consideration.
CONDUCT AT MEETINGS:
Richmond City Council meetings are limited public forums during which the City strives to provide an open, safe atmosphere and promote robust public debate. Members of the public, however, must comply with state law, as well as the City’s laws and procedures and may not actually disrupt the orderly conduct of these meetings. The public, for example, may not shout or use amplifying devices, must submit comment cards and speak during their allotted time, may not create a physical disturbance, may not speak on matters unrelated to issues within the jurisdiction of the City Council or the agenda item at hand, and may not cause immediate threats to public safety.
Click here for City Harassment Policy
Accessibility for Individuals with Disabilities:
Upon request, the City will provide for written agenda materials in appropriate alternative formats, or disability-related modification or accommodation, including auxiliary aids or services and sign language interpreters, to enable individuals with disabilities to participate in and provide comments at/related to public meetings. Please submit a request, including your name, phone number and/or email address, and a description of the modification, accommodation, auxiliary aid, service or alternative format requested at least two days before the meeting.  Requests should be emailed to
[email protected]
or submitted by phone at 510-620-6513, ext. 9, or 510-620-6509.  Requests made by mail to City Clerk’s Office, City Council meeting, 450 Civic Center Plaza, Richmond, CA 94804 must be received at least two days before the meeting. Requests will be granted whenever possible and resolved in favor of accessibility.
Unless otherwise noted in an agenda description, all actions taken by the Council include a determination that CEQA does not apply (i.e., the action is not a project and therefore exempt from CEQA).
Disclaimer:  The City Clerk's Office translates agendas to enhance public access.  Translated agendas are provided as a courtesy and do not constitute legal notice of the meeting or items to be discussed.  In case of discrepancy, the English agenda will prevail.

Claudia Citroen
HALT THE PROJECT IMMEDIATELY!!
Standard Review Request:

Approval of this roadway closure without a complete and legally adequate environmental and planning record exposes the City to CEQA litigation risk, including injunctive relief and potential invalidation.
- CEQA judicial review applies the “abuse of discretion” standard, including:
- Failure to proceed in the manner required by law
- Decisions unsupported by substantial evidence in the administrative record

->INSUFFICIENT EVIDENTIARY RECORD FOR SAFETY FINDINGS
- No quantified collision history provided
- No speed study data provided
- No modeled traffic diversion impacts provided
- No baseline or before/after safety analysis
- Safety improvement claims are unsupported by technical evidence

-> TRAFFIC DIVERSION AND PUBLIC SAFETY IMPACTS
- Closure will shift traffic to surrounding residential and collector streets
- No identification of impacted corridors
- No peak-hour congestion analysis
- No emergency response routing analysis (fire/EMS/police)

-> EMERGENCY ACCESS REQUIREMENTS
- No emergency access study included
- No response time analysis provided
- No confirmation of functional alternate routes for emergency services

-> CALIFORNIA ENVIRONMENTAL QUALITY ACT (CEQA) REQUIREMENTS
CEQA applies to discretionary projects with potential physical environmental change:
- Pub. Resources Code § 21065 et seq.
- CEQA Guidelines § 15000 et seq.
- Roadway closure may affect:
Traffic patterns
Vehicle miles traveled (VMT)
Neighborhood circulation
Air quality and noise distribution
Environmental review or valid exemption must be supported by substantial evidence

-> CEQA “PROJECT” DETERMINATION (REQUIRED)
- Must explicitly determine whether action is a “project” under Pub. Resources Code § 21065
- Determination must be stated on the record before exemption reliance

-> EXEMPTION LIMITATIONS (CEQA GUIDELINES § 15300.2)
Categorical exemptions cannot apply where:
Unusual circumstances exist
Reasonable possibility of significant impacts exists
Cumulative impacts are present
Traffic diversion and emergency access changes may trigger exemption exceptions

-> VEHICLE MILES TRAVELED (VMT) ANALYSIS REQUIREMENT
CEQA Guidelines § 15064.3 requires VMT-based analysis
No VMT analysis provided
Network re-routing effects not evaluated

-> RELIANCE ON 2012 MASTER PLAN / OUTDATED BASELINE
- Environmental review must reflect current conditions, not 2012 assumptions
- No update analysis provided to account for:
Development changes
Traffic pattern shifts
Current roadway usage conditions

->GENERAL PLAN CONSISTENCY REQUIREMENT
- Government Code § 65402 requires explicit consistency findings
- Record does not clearly demonstrate:
Circulation element consistency
Mobility/access consistency
Safety policy alignment
PIECEMEALING PROHIBITION
CEQA prohibits segmentation of related actions
Project must be analyzed as a whole
Related Safe Routes / traffic calming actions may not be isolated

-> CUMULATIVE IMPACTS
- No cumulative analysis for: 
Nearby traffic calming measures
Network-wide circulation changes
Combined congestion/diversion impacts

-> ALTERNATIVES ANALYSIS (CEQA GUIDELINES § 15126.6)
No evaluation of feasible alternatives including:
Partial closure options
Signal timing modifications
Traffic calming without closure
Pilot or reversible design approaches

-> EQUITY AND ACCESS IMPACTS
No analysis of impacts on:
Seniors
Disabled residents
Caregivers
Service providers requiring direct access

-> EMERGENCY SERVICES OPERATIONAL STANDARD
- No documented review by fire, EMS, or police agencies
- No response time impact validation
- No operational routing assessment

-> RECORD ADEQUACY / SUBSTANTIAL EVIDENCE STANDARD
CEQA requires substantial evidence, not conclusions
Safety claims lack supporting:
Engineering studies
Traffic modeling
Environmental analysis

->> CONCLUSION
While safety and multimodal goals are legitimate, the current record is legally and technically insufficient
Approval should be deferred until completion of:
- CEQA determination, analysis
- VMT study
- Traffic and diversion analysis
- General Plan consistency findings
- Emergency services review
- Alternatives analysis
- Updated baseline environmental assessment

-> SUMMARY OF DEFICIENCIES 
The item raises multiple legal and procedural deficiencies: it may expose the City to CEQA liability and potential injunctive relief or invalidation if approval proceeds without proper compliance; it requires an explicit CEQA “project” determination under Pub. Resources Code § 21065; any categorical exemption would be improper if there are unusual circumstances or cumulative impacts under CEQA Guidelines § 15300.2; transportation analysis is deficient because it lacks required vehicle miles traveled (VMT) evaluation under CEQA Guidelines § 15064.3; reliance on a 2012 master plan is legally questionable without updated environmental baseline analysis; General Plan consistency findings are required under Gove
ADOPT a resolution to APPROVE a roadway modification associated with the McBryde Avenue Safe Routes to Park Project, including closure of the direct vehicular connection between Glenn Avenue and McBryde Avenue to improve traffic safety, reduce cut-through traffic, and support pedestrian and bicycle connectivity – Public Works Department (Hillal Hamdan 510-621-1612/Daniel Chavarria 510-620-5478).

Q.3
Contract Amendment No. 2 with Just Cities, LLC for completion of the Housing Equity Roadmap as specified in the Housing Element
Attachments
|
Public Comments
1.
Cont. from 6-16, Just Cities, LLC Amendment No. 2.pdf
2.
Attachment 1 - Just Cities - Amend. No. 2.pdf
3.
Attachment 2 - Original Contract and Amend. No. 1.pdf
Claudia Citroen
Follow up on the initial FORMAL OBJECTION TO CONSENT CALENDAR PLACEMENT W.3.c, REQUEST FOR REMOVAL, AND DEMAND FOR FULL PUBLIC DISCUSSION

The proposed Contract Amendment No. 2 with Just Cities, LLC should be removed from the Consent Calendar and placed on the regular agenda for full public discussion, staff presentation, Council deliberation, and a separate recorded vote.

This item is neither routine nor ministerial. The staff report explicitly states that the Housing Equity Roadmap (HER) will guide future City policies, programs, investments, implementation strategies, housing initiatives, fair housing actions, land-use decisions, and equitable development efforts. These are substantial public policy matters with potentially significant long-term fiscal, legal, and community impacts.

The City seeks to increase the contract by $49,999 and extend the consultant relationship through December 31, 2027, while authorizing continued implementation assistance for policies and recommendations developed by the same consultant. This is not a simple administrative amendment. It represents an expansion of consultant involvement in shaping and advancing future City housing policy.

The staff report further acknowledges that the Housing Equity Roadmap addresses issues including racial segregation, housing production, land-use compatibility, fair housing access, housing habitability, equitable development, and future public investments. These subjects are among the most consequential policy issues facing Richmond and warrant robust public discussion rather than summary approval through a single Consent Calendar vote.

Particularly concerning is the proposed increase of $49,999—an amount that falls precisely one dollar below the commonly recognized $50,000 threshold that often triggers heightened review, reporting, or approval scrutiny under municipal purchasing and contracting practices. While the City may assert compliance with applicable procedures, the proximity of the requested amount to that threshold reasonably raises questions regarding contract structuring, oversight, fiscal transparency, and whether the public is receiving the level of scrutiny expected for expenditures of this nature.

Additionally, Just Cities, LLC prepared the City's original Affirmatively Furthering Fair Housing (AFFH) analysis, subsequently developed the Housing Equity Roadmap, and now seeks additional compensation to assist in implementation of recommendations arising from its own work product. This revolving consultant role raises legitimate questions regarding independent review, performance evaluation, measurable deliverables, consultant accountability, procurement practices, and the absence of competitive alternatives.

Approval of this matter through the Consent Calendar creates the appearance of minimizing public examination of a significant and potentially controversial policy initiative. Such action may be inconsistent with fundamental principles of transparency, accountability, and meaningful public participation that underlie California's open-government framework.

Richmond residents are entitled to understand:

• The total amount paid to Just Cities, LLC under all agreements and amendments;

• The specific deliverables completed to date;

• The measurable outcomes achieved over the previous two years of work;

• The justification for extending the contract through 2027;

• Whether alternative vendors or competitive procurement options were considered;

• What specific implementation activities are contemplated under this amendment;

• What future policy recommendations may result from this work; and

• What fiscal impacts may ultimately arise from implementation of the Housing Equity Roadmap.

The public cannot reasonably evaluate these questions when the matter is buried within the Consent Calendar and voted upon alongside routine administrative items.

Accordingly, residents request that this item be immediately removed from the Consent Calendar and placed on the regular agenda for:

A full staff presentation;
Complete disclosure of prior expenditures and consultant performance;
Public comment and questioning;
Council deliberation on the record; and
A separate roll-call vote.
Major policy initiatives, consultant-driven implementation strategies, and additional expenditures of taxpayer funds deserve full public scrutiny. Transparency requires discussion. Accountability requires deliberation. Public trust requires both.
APPROVE a second contract amendment with Just Cities, LLC to support completion and implementation of the Housing Equity Road Map increasing the payment limit by $49,999, for a total payment limit of $209,994, and extending the term through December 31, 2027– Community Development Department (Lina Velasco 510-620-6841/Jesus Morales 510-620-6649).

ADOPT the recommendations of the Wildfire Preparedness Ad Hoc Committee and DIRECT the City Attorney's Office, in collaboration with the City Administration, to implement the following actions and return to the City Council within 90 days with any necessary ordinances, resolutions, contracts, budget appropriations, and additional implementation plans:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: 2.9% rate increase and new fees at Port of Richmond
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is voting on amendments to Port Tariff No. 3 that would take effect August 1, 2026. The changes include a 2.9% General Rate Increase (GRI) based on the 2026 West Region CPI-U, a new Clean Port Program and Clean Air Program Fee of $200 per hour (replacing the previous per-vehicle Environmental Services Fee), and a full rewrite of the 1994-era tariff to modernize language, remove outdated provisions, and standardize formatting. The $200/hour fee applies to vessels subject to CARB At-Berth Regulations (Title 17, CCR Section 93130) calling at regulated berths, to offset costs of emissions control systems, monitoring, and compliance.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, the new rates and fees apply to all port users starting August 1, 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on accepting $790,693 grant for Richmond Greenway Gap Closure Project
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council is set to vote on accepting a $700,000 Caltrans Sustainable Transportation Planning Grant, plus $90,693 in local Measure J funds, for the Richmond Greenway Gap Closure Project. If approved, the total $790,693 budget would fund planning and preliminary design to close the Greenway Gap at 23rd Street, Carlson Boulevard, and the Union Pacific Railroad tracks with a bicycle and pedestrian bridge. Grant-funded work must be completed by June 30, 2029, with final deliverables due August 29, 2029. The City Manager would be authorized to sign the grant agreement and related documents.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond fire station bond measure on November 2026 ballot
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a resolution to place a $120 million general obligation bond measure on the November 3, 2026 ballot. If approved by at least two-thirds of voters, the bond would fund the repair, reconstruction, and seismic retrofit of aging fire stations (some ~80 years old), renovation of firefighter living quarters including mold and asbestos abatement, and replacement of deteriorating electrical, roofing, and plumbing systems. Debt service would be repaid through an estimated average annual property tax rate of $22.15 per $100,000 of assessed valuation, with a highest estimated rate of $24.06 per $100,000 in 2028. Total estimated debt service including interest is $249,749,206 through 2060.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this bond measure will appear on your November 2026 ballot.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to update ADU and JADU rules to match state law
agenda_briefs.summary: Council held a public hearing and introduced an ordinance amending Richmond Municipal Code sections 15.04.104.020, 15.04.201.030, 15.04.201.050, and 15.04.610.020 to align ADU and JADU regulations with state law. Key changes include: allowing ADU heights up to 16, 18, 20, or 25 feet depending on conditions; eliminating parking standards when an ADU permit is submitted simultaneously with a new single-family or multifamily dwelling permit; permitting at least an 800 sq ft ADU with 4-foot side and rear setbacks (including front yards); allowing JADUs within attached garages; removing the deed restriction requirement for ADUs; allowing one JADU per lot in single-family zones regardless of ADU type; and removing the limitation of one ADU type per lot.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing to voice your support or concerns about the proposed ADU and JADU zoning changes.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on Public Lands Policy
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a resolution establishing a Public Lands Policy requiring project labor agreements for construction on city-owned land. The policy applies to projects with a City's Valuation exceeding $5 million, including leased city land, below-market-rate parcels, parcels with development agreements, and projects receiving city subsidies over $5 million. Developers must negotiate in good faith with the Contra Costa County Building and Construction Trades Council and its affiliated unions.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider prioritizing 23rd Street Ciclovía Pilot Project
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will decide whether to prioritize the 23rd Street Ciclovía Pilot Project, a car-free street event integrated with the Fiestas Patrias Parade & Festival on Saturday, September 12, 2026. The pilot would close 23rd Street between Barrett Avenue and Rheem Avenue from 8:00 AM to 3:00 PM. Preliminary cost estimates range from $79,000 to $145,000 depending on whether barriers are rented or purchased. The decision considers staffing capacity, trade-offs with existing priorities, and impacts on traffic safety, grant-funded projects, and resident requests.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to enforce weed abatement in wildfire hazard zones
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on adopting recommendations from the Wildfire Preparedness Ad Hoc Committee to strengthen enforcement of weed abatement and defensible space rules. A proposed plan would create a faster appeal process for violations in the Local Responsibility Area (LRA) and designated wildfire hazard zones, set up an on-call vegetation management contract for non-compliant parcels, and launch a one-year pilot Resident Assistance Program to help eligible homeowners with improvements within 0–30 feet of structures (priority to Zone 0 hazards). Staff would return within 60 days with an implementation plan, and the City Attorney's Office would evaluate changes to the appeal process within 90 days.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, property owners in wildfire hazard zones may face faster enforcement and new assistance options.

key_dates.label: Implementation plan due to Richmond City Council
Ordinance recommendations due from City Attorney's Office

agenda_briefs.source_body:
Q.5
Implementing Wildfire Taskforce Recommendation Regarding Enforcing Weed Abatement Ordinance
ADOPT the recommendations of the Wildfire Preparedness Ad Hoc Committee and DIRECT the City Attorney's Office, in collaboration with the City Administration, to implement the following actions and return to the City Council within 90 days with any necessary ordinances, resolutions, contracts, budget appropriations, and additional implementation plans:
Defensible Space Appeal Process
Direct the City Attorney's Office to evaluate the current appeal process for enforcement of defensible space violations under the Richmond Municipal Code, including whether appeal timelines can be shortened for parcels located within the Local Responsibility Area (LRA) and the City's designated wildfire hazard zones, and return to the City Council with recommendations for any necessary amendments to the Richmond Municipal Code.
Vegetation Management Contract
Direct staff to establish an on-call vegetation management services contract for parcels within wildfire hazard zones where property owners fail to comply with defensible space requirements following all applicable enforcement procedures, and determine the appropriate department to administer and oversee the contract.
Resident Assistance Program
Direct staff to develop a one-year pilot Resident Assistance Program to assist eligible homeowners with weed abatement ordinance and defensible space improvements within 0–30 feet of structures, with priority given to hazards located within Zone 0, and return to the City Council with program guidelines, eligibility criteria, funding options, and implementation recommendations.
Implementation Report
Direct staff to return to the City Council within 60 days with an implementation plan, including proposed ordinances or policy amendments, estimated costs, potential funding sources, staffing requirements, and a proposed implementation schedule for each recommendation – Councilmember Soheila Bana (510-620-6743)/Councilmember Cesar Zepeda (510-620-6593).

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond GATE Act ballot initiative for independent ethics commission
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is considering a ballot initiative for the November 2026 election to establish an independent Ethics Commission with a dedicated Investigator, subpoena power, and an absolute minimum budget floor of $1,000,000 in Year 1 (rising to at least 0.5% of General Fund revenues in Year 2). The Commission would have jurisdiction over elected officials, appointed officials, commissioners, lobbyists, contractors, and nonprofits doing business with the City. Council will hear a presentation and vote on directing staff to prepare the ordinance and ballot measure documents. A final vote to place the measure on the ballot must occur by July 28, 2026.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the July 21 council meeting or submit comments before the July 28 ballot deadline to voice support or concerns.
